subtopic_detail: Postmenopausal IDA mandates upper and lower GI endoscopy
stem: A 64-year-old postmenopausal woman attends her GP with three months of progressive fatigue and exertional dyspnoea climbing two flights of stairs. She has no overt PR bleeding, no melaena and no haematemesis but notes mild epigastric discomfort relieved by food. She takes ibuprofen 400 mg PRN for osteoarthritis (about three times per week) and is otherwise well. She has never had a colonoscopy. Her father died of colorectal cancer at 67. She does not drink alcohol and has never smoked. Her diet includes red meat twice weekly. There is no overt haemorrhage source on history. Examination shows conjunctival pallor without jaundice or lymphadenopathy. Abdomen is soft with mild epigastric tenderness, no masses, PR exam reveals no haemorrhoids and the stool sample sent for FOBT is positive. Coeliac serology is pending. She has not had bariatric surgery and is not vegetarian. She wants to know what investigation is most appropriate now.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, T 36.7 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival pallor, koilonychia, no lymphadenopathy, abdomen soft with mild epigastric tenderness, PR no masses, FOBT positive.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 86 g/L, MCV 71 fL, MCH 23 pg, ferritin 6 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 8%, CRP 4 mg/L, eGFR 78 mL/min/1.73m^2, coeliac serology pending.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Capsule endoscopy of the small bowel
options.rationale: Capsule endoscopy is reserved for obscure GI bleeding after negative bidirectional endoscopy; it is not first-line in a postmenopausal woman with IDA and a positive FOBT (BSG IDA Guideline 2021; eviQ has no capsule endoscopy pathway for primary IDA).

options.text: Oral ferrous sulfate trial and reassess Hb in three months
options.rationale: Empirical iron without source identification is inappropriate in this risk group; delay risks missing colorectal cancer that this presentation strongly suggests (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).

options.text: CT colonography as first-line in place of colonoscopy
options.rationale: CTC is an alternative only if colonoscopy is contraindicated or incomplete; it cannot biopsy or treat lesions and misses upper GI pathology, which must still be evaluated separately (NHMRC Colorectal Cancer Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

options.text: Repeat FBC and ferritin in 6 weeks before any endoscopic referral
options.rationale: Ferritin 6 micrograms/L is definitively diagnostic of iron deficiency; repeating bloods delays cancer workup and is not indicated (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).

options.text: Bidirectional upper and lower GI endoscopy at the same session
options.rationale: Postmenopausal women and all men with confirmed IDA require bidirectional endoscopy to exclude an occult GI malignancy or lesion; ferritin 6 with microcytic anaemia and a positive FOBT in a 64-year-old with a family history of colorectal cancer is a category 1 indication (BSG IDA Guideline 2021; NHMRC Colorectal Cancer Guidelines).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal women and adult men with confirmed iron deficiency anaemia require bidirectional endoscopy at the same session to exclude GI malignancy, regardless of NSAID exposure or overt symptoms (BSG IDA Guideline 2021).
key_points: Ferritin <30 micrograms/L confirms iron deficiency; <15 highly specific
Bidirectional endoscopy is category 1 in postmenopausal women and all men
NSAID use does not negate the need for colonoscopy
Coeliac serology should be checked in parallel
explanation.pearls: FOBT positivity in confirmed IDA does NOT make upper endoscopy unnecessary - dual workup remains standard.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Guidelines

subtopic_detail: Subacute combined degeneration - parenteral B12 indicated
stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with six months of progressive fatigue and three months of paraesthesiae in both feet that have ascended to mid-calf. She reports unsteadiness in the dark and a recent fall when reaching for an upper shelf. She has type 1 diabetes for 20 years on basal-bolus insulin, autoimmune hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 micrograms and takes metformin 1 g BD for adjunctive insulin resistance. She has been on long-term proton pump inhibitor for reflux. She does not drink alcohol and is not vegan. She has had no bariatric surgery. On examination she has a beefy red tongue, brisk knee jerks but absent ankle jerks, impaired joint position and vibration sense to the mid-shins and a positive Romberg. There is no anaemic murmur. The GP requests urgent bloods. The MMSE is 28/30 with reduced recall. She is independently mobile with a four-wheel walker she has been using for the last month.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Beefy red tongue, glossitis, conjunctival pallor; absent ankle jerks, brisk knee jerks, reduced JPS and vibration to mid-shins, positive Romberg; no peripheral neuropathy of stocking distribution beyond mid-calf.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, MCV 114 fL, WCC 4.2, platelets 142, hypersegmented neutrophils on blood film, serum B12 78 pmol/L (RR >150), red cell folate 480 nmol/L, TSH 2.4, ferritin 145, LDH elevated, anti-intrinsic factor antibody positive.
data_table.Medications: Insulin glargine + aspart, metformin 1 g BD, levothyroxine 75 mcg, esomeprazole 20 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Oral cyanocobalamin 1000 micrograms daily indefinitely and review symptoms in clinic in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Oral therapy is acceptable for asymptomatic dietary B12 deficiency but is NOT appropriate for pernicious anaemia with neurological involvement; parenteral loading is required to prevent permanent neurological damage (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014; AMH).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM on alternate days until improvement plateaus, then 1 mg every 2 months
options.rationale: For B12 deficiency with neurological features (SACD), hydroxocobalamin 1 mg IM is given on alternate days until no further improvement, then 2-monthly maintenance for life in pernicious anaemia. Anti-intrinsic factor antibody confirms pernicious anaemia (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014; AMH).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Start oral folic acid 5 mg daily while awaiting the Schilling test or holotranscobalamin level
options.rationale: Folate replacement in unrecognised B12 deficiency can precipitate or worsen SACD; the Schilling test is obsolete and no longer performed in Australia (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Cease the metformin and recheck the B12 level at routine follow-up in 3 months
options.rationale: Metformin can reduce B12 absorption but is not the primary driver here given positive anti-intrinsic factor antibody; stopping metformin alone does not treat established SACD (BCSH B12/Folate Guideline 2014).
source_refs: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today given the new neurological symptoms and anaemia
options.rationale: Hb 96 g/L in a haemodynamically stable patient does not meet transfusion threshold; replacement of B12 corrects the underlying deficit and transfusion does not address neurology (ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

explanation.summary: Pernicious anaemia with subacute combined degeneration requires prompt parenteral hydroxocobalamin loading (alternate days until response plateaus) then 2-monthly maintenance for life. Folate alone risks precipitating SACD (BCSH 2014; AMH).
key_points: Neurological B12 deficiency = parenteral loading, NOT oral
Anti-intrinsic factor antibody is highly specific for pernicious anaemia
Folate before B12 can worsen neurology
PPI and metformin both impair B12 absorption
explanation.pearls: Brisk knee jerks + absent ankle jerks + sensory ataxia = SACD until proven otherwise.

sources.label: BCSH B12 and Folate Deficiency Guidelines
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

stem: A 68-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate 20 mg weekly and prednisolone 5 mg daily presents with worsening fatigue. She had a flare two months ago managed with a prednisolone pulse. She has no overt bleeding, no melaena, no menstruation since age 52 and a normal colonoscopy three years ago. She has no chronic kidney disease (eGFR 76) and no malignancy. Her appetite is preserved and BMI is 26 kg/m^2. She takes paracetamol regularly and avoids NSAIDs because of methotrexate. She has no overt source of blood loss. On examination she has active synovitis of MCPs and wrists with mild conjunctival pallor and no organomegaly. There is no telangiectasia and no hepatomegaly. She lives independently and is a retired nurse. Her ESR is 78 mm/h and CRP 42 mg/L.

data_table.Examination: Active synovitis MCPs/wrists, conjunctival pallor, no hepatosplenomegaly or lymphadenopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 98 g/L, MCV 84 fL (normocytic), ferritin 142 micrograms/L, transferrin saturation 12%, serum iron 6 micromol/L (RR 10-30), TIBC 38 micromol/L, CRP 42 mg/L, ESR 78, soluble transferrin receptor elevated, reticulocyte index low, B12 and folate normal.

lead_in: Which result pattern most strongly indicates that iron deficiency is contributing in addition to anaemia of chronic disease?

options.text: Normocytic indices with ferritin 142 micrograms/L in isolation
options.rationale: Ferritin 142 in isolation suggests adequate stores, but in active inflammation ferritin is an acute-phase reactant and can be falsely normal; this finding alone does NOT identify combined deficiency (BCSH IDA Guideline; eTG Haematology).

options.text: Low serum iron with normal TIBC and elevated ESR
options.rationale: Low serum iron with normal/reduced TIBC is the classic ACD pattern alone; this does not establish combined iron deficiency without additional markers (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Hb 98 g/L with active synovitis, suggesting any anaemia in this context must be iron deficiency
options.rationale: ACD alone can produce a normocytic anaemia at Hb 90-110 g/L; clinical inflammation is not specific for combined IDA without confirmatory iron studies (Therapeutic Guidelines: Haematology).

options.text: Transferrin saturation 12% AND elevated soluble transferrin receptor
options.rationale: In active inflammation ferritin is unreliable; transferrin saturation under 20% with elevated soluble transferrin receptor (which is NOT acute-phase modified) reliably indicates true iron deficiency superimposed on ACD. This pattern justifies iron replacement, ideally parenteral given inflammation reduces oral absorption (BSG IDA 2021; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: MCV 84 fL and low reticulocyte index
options.rationale: Normocytic indices with hypoproliferative reticulocytes are characteristic of ACD itself; this pattern does not differentiate combined IDA from pure ACD (BCSH IDA Guideline 2021).

explanation.summary: In active inflammation, ferritin is acute-phase elevated and unreliable for diagnosing iron deficiency. Transferrin saturation under 20% with elevated soluble transferrin receptor (not affected by inflammation) is the most reliable combined-deficiency signature (BSG IDA 2021).
key_points: Ferritin <100 micrograms/L in active inflammation may still indicate iron deficiency
Transferrin saturation <20% supports iron deficiency
Soluble transferrin receptor is NOT an acute-phase reactant
Parenteral iron preferred when oral absorption is impaired by inflammation
explanation.pearls: In rheumatoid arthritis with CRP >10, use ferritin cut-off of 100 (not 30) before excluding iron deficiency.

stem: A 58-year-old woman presents with 10 days of progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and dark urine. She noticed mild scleral icterus three days ago. She has no fever, no weight loss and no recent infections. Her past history includes well-controlled SLE on hydroxychloroquine 200 mg BD. She does not take new medications. She has no history of transfusion. Examination reveals pallor, mild scleral icterus, no lymphadenopathy and a 2 cm palpable spleen tip below the costal margin. There is no cold-induced acrocyanosis or livedo. There are no thrombotic features and no haematuria. She is haemodynamically stable on the ward, not in distress. The lab phones with critical results showing a positive direct antiglobulin test (DAT) strongly positive for IgG and weakly positive for C3d, raised reticulocytes, elevated LDH, low haptoglobin and unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia. Blood film shows spherocytes and polychromasia. She has no significant cardiac or renal disease. She wants to know how this will be treated.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 102 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 97%, T 36.8 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Pale, scleral icterus, 2 cm palpable spleen tip, no lymphadenopathy, no acrocyanosis.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 72 g/L, MCV 102 fL, reticulocytes 11%, LDH 820 U/L, haptoglobin <0.1 g/L, unconjugated bilirubin 58 micromol/L, DAT IgG strongly positive C3d weakly positive, blood film spherocytes and polychromasia, cold agglutinin titre negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Urgent splenectomy
options.rationale: Splenectomy is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing warm AIHA, not first-line; vaccination against encapsulated organisms is required prior (BSH AIHA 2017).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

options.text: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day orally with monitoring of Hb and reticulocytes
options.rationale: First-line therapy for warm AIHA is prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day until response, then taper over weeks to months; about 80% achieve initial response. Add folate replacement and PJP prophylaxis if high-dose steroid prolonged (BCSH AIHA Guideline; eTG Haematology).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

options.text: Plasma exchange with fresh frozen plasma replacement
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is used in TTP and severe cold agglutinin disease with hyperviscosity, not warm AIHA; cold agglutinin titre here is negative (BSH AIHA 2017; ANZSBT).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Avoid all blood transfusion regardless of severity
options.rationale: Transfusion is not absolutely contraindicated; least incompatible units may be given for life-threatening anaemia with close clinical and lab monitoring (ANZSBT; BSH AIHA 2017).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products
BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

options.text: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly for 4 weeks as monotherapy
options.rationale: Rituximab is increasingly used as second-line or in combination with steroids, but monotherapy is not the standard first-line approach in newly diagnosed warm AIHA (BSH AIHA 2017).
source_refs: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017

explanation.summary: Warm AIHA (DAT IgG positive, often C3d weak) is treated first-line with prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. Splenectomy and rituximab are second-line. Cold agglutinin disease (DAT C3d only, cold titre high) is managed differently (BSH AIHA 2017).
key_points: DAT IgG positive = warm AIHA
First-line: prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day
Add folate; consider PJP prophylaxis if prolonged steroids
Transfusion with least-incompatible units if life-threatening
explanation.pearls: Warm AIHA in SLE is the prototype secondary warm AIHA - control the underlying disease too.

sources.label: BSH Guidelines on AIHA in Adults 2017
ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

subtopic_detail: Indications for red cell exchange transfusion
stem: A 28-year-old man with homozygous HbSS sickle cell disease presents with three days of bilateral pleuritic chest pain, fever and dyspnoea. He was admitted six days ago for a typical vaso-occlusive crisis of the lower limbs and has been on regular morphine PCA, IV fluids and incentive spirometry. He has had four prior admissions for VOC this year and one previous episode of acute chest syndrome two years ago. He is fully vaccinated including pneumococcal and is on penicillin V prophylaxis. He has no known allergies. He is normally on hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg daily but adherence has been poor. He works as a delivery driver. On examination he is in respiratory distress, with new bilateral basal crepitations and oxygen saturation of 88% on room air, improving to 93% on 6 L via Hudson mask. There is no calf swelling. CXR shows new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates. Hb has dropped from baseline 88 to 64 g/L. He has a leucocytosis with WCC 22 and CRP 180. He is started on IV ceftriaxone and azithromycin.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.9 deg C, HR 124 bpm, RR 28, BP 102/64 mmHg, SpO2 88% RA, 93% on 6 L O2.
data_table.Examination: Respiratory distress, bilateral basal crepitations, no focal lower limb signs.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 64 g/L (baseline 88), HbS 78%, WCC 22 x10^9/L, platelets 280, reticulocytes 14%, CRP 180, troponin negative, ABG pH 7.36 pCO2 38 pO2 58 on 6 L, CXR new bilateral lower lobe infiltrates, D-dimer 4.2.
data_table.Medications: Morphine PCA, IV fluids, ceftriaxone, azithromycin, hydroxyurea 20 mg/kg (poor adherence).

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Simple top-up transfusion of 1 unit packed red cells
options.rationale: Simple transfusion may be used in mild ACS but is inadequate for severe ACS with hypoxia and hyperdense HbS load; it does not effectively reduce HbS fraction and risks hyperviscosity (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Empirical therapeutic dose enoxaparin for presumed pulmonary embolism
options.rationale: While PE must be considered, the bilateral infiltrates, fever, pleuritic pain and HbS load with falling Hb make ACS the primary process; full anticoagulation without CTPA risks haemorrhage and does not treat the underlying sickling (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Urgent red cell exchange transfusion targeting HbS less than 30%
options.rationale: Severe acute chest syndrome (hypoxia despite supplemental O2, bilateral infiltrates, falling Hb from baseline, multilobar involvement) is an indication for urgent red cell exchange aiming HbS <30%. This rapidly reduces sickling, improves oxygenation and reduces mortality (BSH ACS Guideline 2015; HSANZ).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015
HSANZ position statement on sickle cell disease

options.text: Continue current oxygen, fluids and antibiotics with hourly observations only
options.rationale: Conservative management alone is inadequate for severe ACS with hypoxaemia and falling Hb; mortality without transfusion intervention is substantial (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

options.text: Increase hydroxyurea to 35 mg/kg/day to drive HbF production
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea is a chronic disease-modifying therapy that takes weeks to months to raise HbF and reduce crises; it has no role in acute management of severe ACS (BSH 2015).
source_refs: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015

explanation.summary: Severe acute chest syndrome requires urgent red cell exchange to reduce HbS to under 30%, alongside broad-spectrum antibiotics covering atypicals, supplemental oxygen, analgesia and adequate (but not excessive) IV fluids. Mortality is high without prompt exchange (BSH ACS Guideline 2015).
key_points: Severe ACS = hypoxia despite supplemental O2 or multilobar infiltrates
Exchange transfusion targets HbS <30%
Antibiotic cover for typical AND atypical pathogens
Continue analgesia and incentive spirometry
explanation.pearls: VOC + new respiratory signs + new infiltrate = ACS; treat early, transfuse fast.

sources.label: BSH Sickle Cell Disease in Adults Guideline 2015
HSANZ position statement on sickle cell disease

stem: A 74-year-old woman attends ED with one day of right-sided pleuritic chest pain and mild dyspnoea on exertion. She has hypertension on perindopril and osteoarthritis. She had a knee arthroplasty 8 weeks ago and has otherwise been active. She has no haemoptysis, no calf swelling, no recent immobilisation beyond the post-op period and no malignancy. She does not smoke. On examination she is comfortable at rest, with clear chest, no calf tenderness or asymmetry and oxygen saturation 96% on room air. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 102 bpm. The intern calculates a Wells score of 1.5 (recent surgery within 4 weeks: 1.5) - PE unlikely (less than or equal to 4). CXR is normal. The intern asks whether she needs a CTPA, a D-dimer, or both, given her age. She has no contraindication to contrast and her eGFR is 68. She has no known thrombophilia. She is asking about her risk of getting an unnecessary scan.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 102 bpm, BP 138/82 mmHg, RR 18, SpO2 96% RA, T 36.7 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Clear chest, no calf signs, comfortable at rest.
data_table.Investigations: Troponin negative, ECG sinus tachycardia, CXR normal, eGFR 68, FBC normal, Wells score 1.5 (PE unlikely).

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed directly to CTPA given her age and recent surgery
options.rationale: Wells unlikely with a negative age-adjusted D-dimer safely excludes PE without CTPA; going straight to CTPA exposes her to contrast and radiation unnecessarily (eTG; BTS PE Guideline).

options.text: Use the standard fixed D-dimer cut-off of 500 micrograms/L regardless of age
options.rationale: Fixed cut-offs over-investigate older patients; age-adjusted D-dimer maintains specificity while preserving sensitivity in Wells-unlikely patients over 50 (ADJUST-PE; eTG).

options.text: Start therapeutic anticoagulation empirically and image within 24 hours
options.rationale: Empirical anticoagulation is for high pre-test probability patients awaiting imaging that is delayed beyond 4 hours; this patient is Wells-unlikely (eTG; BTS).

options.text: Send age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 micrograms/L); if below 740 micrograms/L exclude PE
options.rationale: In Wells PE-unlikely patients over 50, age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 micrograms/L FEU; for this patient 740) safely excludes PE without imaging. If above this cut-off, proceed to CTPA (eTG; ADJUST-PE study; BTS PE).

options.text: Perform V/Q scan as first-line because CXR is normal
options.rationale: V/Q is preferred when contrast is contraindicated (renal impairment, pregnancy, contrast allergy); first-line in standard adults with adequate renal function is age-adjusted D-dimer then CTPA if positive (eTG).

explanation.summary: In Wells PE-unlikely patients (score <= 4), age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 micrograms/L FEU for those over 50) safely excludes PE without CTPA. A negative result has high NPV; if positive, proceed to imaging (eTG; ADJUST-PE).
key_points: Wells <= 4 = PE unlikely; use D-dimer
Age-adjusted cut-off (age x 10) above age 50
Wells > 4 = PE likely; go to CTPA directly
V/Q reserved for contrast contraindications
explanation.pearls: The Wells 'PE most likely diagnosis' criterion is 3 points and the most subjective - apply judiciously.

stem: A 67-year-old man with newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal cancer (liver metastases) presents with a confirmed unprovoked left iliofemoral DVT and segmental subsegmental PE on CTPA. He started FOLFOX chemotherapy 10 days ago. He is haemodynamically stable, normotensive and not hypoxic. He has chronic kidney disease with eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, no liver dysfunction (bilirubin 16, ALT 28), and no prior GI bleeding. He has a colostomy after primary resection. There is no upper GI lesion documented on endoscopy 3 months ago. He has no thrombocytopenia (platelets 188) and no anaemia. He is not on antiplatelets. He has no history of intracranial haemorrhage. He weighs 78 kg. He is on ondansetron for nausea, dexamethasone PRN with chemotherapy, paracetamol for analgesia and atorvastatin 40 mg. He is keen to take an oral agent and avoid daily injections if possible. He understands that anticoagulation must continue for at least the duration of active malignancy. He has no platelet transfusion history and no autoimmune disease.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, SpO2 97% RA.
data_table.Examination: Left leg swelling to mid-thigh, palpable colostomy site, no jaundice.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 118 g/L, platelets 188, eGFR 38, bilirubin 16, ALT 28, INR 1.0, no upper GI lesion on endoscopy 3 months ago.
data_table.Medications: FOLFOX chemotherapy, ondansetron, dexamethasone PRN, paracetamol, atorvastatin 40 mg.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate anticoagulation choice?

options.text: Rivaroxaban 15 mg BD for 21 days then 20 mg daily
options.rationale: Rivaroxaban is acceptable for non-luminal cancer VTE but has a higher GI bleed risk than apixaban in CAT and is less preferred when eGFR is borderline; the standard 20 mg daily dose is also problematic when eGFR is 30-49 (eviQ; AMH).

options.text: Dabigatran 150 mg BD after 5 days of LMWH lead-in
options.rationale: Dabigatran requires a parenteral lead-in and is contraindicated when CrCl is under 30 with limited data at CrCl 30-50; not first-line in CAT (AMH; eviQ).

options.text: Warfarin bridged with LMWH until INR 2-3 stable
options.rationale: Warfarin is inferior to LMWH and DOACs in CAT due to interactions with chemo and erratic INR control during nausea/vomiting; reserved for when DOACs and LMWH cannot be used (eviQ).

options.text: Apixaban 10 mg BD for 7 days then 5 mg BD
options.rationale: In cancer-associated thrombosis without luminal GI malignancy and with eGFR over 30, apixaban (preferred for renal impairment among DOACs) or LMWH are first-line; apixaban has the lowest GI bleed signal of DOACs and meets the patient's oral-preference goal (eviQ Cancer-associated thrombosis; eTG).

options.text: Aspirin 100 mg daily as secondary prevention
options.rationale: Aspirin is NOT acceptable monotherapy for acute VTE; it is only considered for extended prevention after initial 3-6 months full anticoagulation in selected patients (eTG).

explanation.summary: For cancer-associated thrombosis without luminal GI or GU malignancy, apixaban or LMWH are first-line. Apixaban has the lowest bleeding signal of DOACs and is preferred when eGFR is 30-49 (eviQ; eTG).
key_points: Luminal GI/GU cancer = caution with DOACs; consider LMWH
Apixaban dose: 10 mg BD x 7 days then 5 mg BD
Continue anticoagulation while cancer active
Avoid dabigatran when CrCl is borderline
explanation.pearls: CAT recurrence is highest in the first 3 months - don't switch agents prematurely.

stem: A 72-year-old man is on day 8 post coronary artery bypass grafting. He has been receiving prophylactic enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily since post-op day 1 plus an unfractionated heparin infusion for 48 hours intraoperatively and post-op. He develops new right calf swelling, tenderness and a confirmed proximal DVT on ultrasound. His platelet count has fallen from 280 x10^9/L pre-op to 78 x10^9/L today, a nadir 72% drop. There is no bleeding. He has no skin necrosis at injection sites and no thrombocytopenia of other obvious cause - no sepsis, no DIC, no recent IV contrast. The blood film shows no schistocytes. The cardiothoracic registrar calculates a 4Ts score of 7 (thrombocytopenia 2, timing 2, thrombosis 2, no other cause 1). The lab confirms PF4-heparin ELISA is strongly positive and a serotonin release assay is sent. He has CKD with eGFR 42.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 37.0 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Right calf swollen and tender, no skin necrosis, no bleeding, sternotomy wound clean.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 78 x10^9/L (pre-op 280), Hb 102 g/L, INR 1.1, fibrinogen 4.2 g/L, no schistocytes, PF4 antibody strongly positive ELISA, 4Ts 7 (high probability), eGFR 42.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Stop heparin and observe expectantly; restart the heparin infusion once platelets have recovered fully
options.rationale: Stopping alone leaves the patient in a prothrombotic state with high risk of new venous and arterial events; rechallenge with heparin is contraindicated (ASH 2018).

options.text: Continue enoxaparin at therapeutic dose and add aspirin 100 mg daily for additional antiplatelet cover
options.rationale: All heparins (including LMWH) must be stopped immediately when HIT is suspected at a high probability score; aspirin is inadequate (ASH 2018).

options.text: Start warfarin immediately to bridge through the heparin withdrawal phase with target INR 2-3
options.rationale: Warfarin must NOT be started in acute HIT until platelets recover above 150 because of risk of warfarin-induced skin necrosis from protein C depletion (ASH 2018).

options.text: Transfuse platelets to correct the thrombocytopenia and reduce the risk of major spontaneous haemorrhage
options.rationale: Prophylactic platelet transfusion is contraindicated in HIT unless major bleeding is occurring, because it can worsen thrombosis (ASH 2018; ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Stop all heparin AND start a non-heparin anticoagulant (fondaparinux or argatroban) at therapeutic dose
options.rationale: HIT is a prothrombotic, life-threatening condition. Stopping heparin alone is INSUFFICIENT - patients require therapeutic-dose non-heparin anticoagulation. Fondaparinux is acceptable off-label in stable patients; argatroban is preferred in renal/critically ill (ASH HIT Guideline 2018; eTG).

explanation.summary: HIT is a clinical-laboratory diagnosis with a high 4Ts score plus a positive PF4 antibody confirmed by functional assay. Stop all heparin (UFH and LMWH) AND substitute a non-heparin therapeutic anticoagulant; do not give warfarin or platelets in acute phase (ASH 2018).
key_points: 4Ts 0-3 low, 4-5 intermediate, 6-8 high probability
Stop all heparin AND substitute therapeutic non-heparin agent
Avoid warfarin in acute HIT until platelets > 150
Avoid prophylactic platelet transfusion
explanation.pearls: Timing nadir 5-10 days post heparin exposure is classic; rapid-onset HIT (<24 hours) occurs with recent prior exposure within 100 days.

sources.label: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with one week of widespread mucocutaneous bruising and gum bleeding when brushing her teeth. She has had two episodes of epistaxis lasting under 10 minutes each. She denies haemarthrosis or melaena. She is otherwise well, takes no medications, has no recent vaccinations and no recent viral illness. She is not pregnant. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. On examination she is haemodynamically stable. She has petechiae on the lower limbs and buccal mucosa, scattered bruising on the limbs and gingival oozing. There is no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. There are no rashes suggestive of vasculitis. Bloods reveal isolated thrombocytopenia at 8 x10^9/L with normal WCC, normal differential, normal Hb, normal coagulation profile, normal renal and liver function. Blood film shows large platelets, no schistocytes, no blasts. HIV, hepatitis B/C, and Helicobacter pylori serology are negative. ANA is negative. She has no contraindication to corticosteroids and no recent infections. She works as a primary school teacher and is keen to avoid admission if safe.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Petechiae lower limbs and buccal mucosa, bruising, gingival oozing, no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 8 x10^9/L, Hb 132, WCC 6.4 normal differential, INR 1.0, APTT 30, blood film large platelets no blasts, HIV/HBV/HCV negative, ANA negative.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Platelet transfusion of 2 adult therapeutic doses
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion is reserved for life-threatening bleeding (intracranial, major GI); transfused platelets are rapidly consumed by the same antibody and do not raise count meaningfully (ASH 2019; ANZSBT).
source_refs: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

options.text: Prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily for 1-2 weeks then taper
options.rationale: First-line therapy in adult ITP with bleeding or platelets under 20-30 is corticosteroids; prednisolone 1 mg/kg or short high-dose dexamethasone (40 mg daily x 4 days) are both accepted. Response in days to weeks (ASH ITP Guideline 2019; eTG).

options.text: Rituximab 375 mg/m^2 weekly for 4 weeks
options.rationale: Rituximab is second-line for steroid-refractory or relapsing ITP, not first-line (ASH 2019).

options.text: Splenectomy
options.rationale: Splenectomy is second-line, usually after 12 months of relapsing disease to allow for spontaneous remission and after pneumococcal/meningococcal/Hib vaccination (ASH 2019).

options.text: Bone marrow biopsy before any treatment
options.rationale: Bone marrow is not required in typical adult ITP with isolated thrombocytopenia and a normal blood film; reserve for atypical features or age over 60 (ASH 2019).

explanation.summary: Adult ITP first-line is corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg or short dexamethasone pulse). IVIG is added when more rapid platelet rise is needed (active bleeding, procedure imminent). Platelet transfusion only for life-threatening bleeding (ASH 2019).
key_points: First-line: corticosteroids
Add IVIG 1 g/kg if rapid response needed
Anti-D in Rh-positive non-splenectomised patients - alternative
Treatment threshold: platelets <30 or bleeding
explanation.pearls: Avoid platelet transfusion in stable ITP - the antibodies eat them in hours.

sources.label: ANZSBT Guidelines for the Administration of Blood Products

subtopic_detail: Urgent FBC + blood film + bone marrow
stem: A 46-year-old woman presents to her GP with three weeks of progressive fatigue, easy bruising of the limbs, gum bleeding and a low-grade fever. She had two episodes of unexplained epistaxis lasting 15 minutes each. She has no past medical history of note and takes no medications. She has lost 3 kg unintentionally in the last month and has drenching night sweats. She has no foreign travel and no contact with TB. On examination she is pale, with widespread bruising and petechiae, gum hypertrophy and bleeding, a 4 cm palpable spleen and shotty cervical lymphadenopathy. Bloods at her GP show pancytopenia: Hb 76 g/L, WCC 38 x10^9/L (with 70% blasts on differential), platelets 14 x10^9/L. Blood film reports myeloid blasts with Auer rods. Coagulation profile shows fibrinogen 1.0 g/L (low) and a raised D-dimer. She has not received transfusion. She is haemodynamically stable but the GP is concerned about possible acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML). She has no current bleeding source other than gums.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 37.6 deg C.
data_table.Examination: Pale, widespread bruising and petechiae, gum hypertrophy and bleeding, 4 cm splenomegaly, shotty cervical nodes.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 76 g/L, WCC 38 x10^9/L (70% blasts with Auer rods), platelets 14 x10^9/L, INR 1.6, fibrinogen 1.0 g/L, D-dimer 6.5, LDH 980, urate 0.52 mmol/L.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Discharge home with routine outpatient haematology referral in 2 weeks for further workup
options.rationale: Suspected APML with coagulopathy is a haematological emergency with high early mortality; outpatient referral is unsafe (eviQ).

options.text: Start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics and observe in ED for 24 hours before specialist referral
options.rationale: Antibiotics may be needed if febrile neutropenia develops, but they must not delay urgent haematology transfer and ATRA in suspected APML (eviQ).

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells and 1 pool of platelets then reassess clinically
options.rationale: Transfusion support may be appropriate but is not the SINGLE next step - definitive treatment and rapid haematology transfer are the priority (ANZSBT; eviQ).

options.text: Same-day transfer to haematology for urgent bone marrow biopsy with empirical ATRA if APML suspected
options.rationale: Acute leukaemia with coagulopathy (low fibrinogen, raised D-dimer) and Auer rods on film highly suggests APML; empirical all-trans retinoic acid (ATRA) is started on clinical suspicion (do not wait for confirmation) because of bleeding-death risk. Urgent transfer for bone marrow, cytogenetics and PML-RARA testing (eviQ; BSH AML Guideline).

options.text: Repeat the full blood count in 48 hours to confirm the pancytopenia before any specialist referral
options.rationale: Pancytopenia with circulating blasts is diagnostic of leukaemia until proven otherwise; delay is unsafe (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Acute leukaemia with Auer rods plus coagulopathy is APML until proven otherwise. Same-day transfer to a haematology centre and empirical ATRA initiation reduce early haemorrhagic death (eviQ; BSH AML Guideline).
key_points: Blasts + Auer rods + coagulopathy = APML
ATRA empirically on suspicion - do not wait for confirmation
Same-day haematology transfer
Aggressive blood product support during induction
explanation.pearls: APML is the most curable acute leukaemia but early death from coagulopathy is the killer - ATRA is started before cytogenetics confirm PML-RARA.

stem: A 48-year-old man is referred from his GP after a routine FBC for life insurance showed a leucocytosis. He feels well other than mild left upper quadrant discomfort and early satiety. He has no fevers, no night sweats and no weight loss. He has no past medical history of note and takes no medications. On examination there is a 6 cm splenomegaly palpable below the costal margin. There is no lymphadenopathy. The lab phones the GP with results showing WCC 86 x10^9/L with a full spectrum of myeloid maturation on the blood film (myelocytes, metamyelocytes, basophilia, eosinophilia), no excess blasts (less than 2%), Hb 124 g/L, platelets 480 x10^9/L. He is haemodynamically stable. He has no infections and no organomegaly other than the spleen. Bone marrow aspirate is hypercellular with myeloid hyperplasia and 1% blasts. Cytogenetics confirm t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 transcript by PCR. He has no cardiac history and no other comorbidities. He works as an accountant.

data_table.Examination: 6 cm splenomegaly, no lymphadenopathy, no other organomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: WCC 86 x10^9/L with myeloid left shift, basophilia 5%, eosinophilia 4%, blasts <2%, Hb 124 g/L, platelets 480 x10^9/L; bone marrow hypercellular myeloid 1% blasts; cytogenetics t(9;22) and BCR-ABL1 positive.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Allogeneic stem cell transplant as first-line
options.rationale: Allogeneic transplant is reserved for accelerated/blast phase or TKI-failure cases; first-line transplant in chronic phase is no longer standard given TKI efficacy (eviQ CML).

options.text: Hydroxyurea 1-2 g daily until disease control
options.rationale: Hydroxyurea controls counts but does not affect underlying BCR-ABL biology; used as a bridge before TKI in symptomatic hyperleucocytosis, not as definitive therapy (eviQ CML).

options.text: Interferon alpha monotherapy
options.rationale: Interferon was superseded by TKIs; reserved now for pregnancy or rare TKI intolerance (eviQ).

options.text: ATRA plus arsenic trioxide
options.rationale: ATRA plus arsenic is the treatment for acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML), not CML (eviQ APML).

options.text: Imatinib 400 mg orally daily
options.rationale: First-line treatment for chronic phase CML is a BCR-ABL1 tyrosine kinase inhibitor; imatinib 400 mg daily is standard with 10-year survival approaching that of the general population. Nilotinib and dasatinib are alternatives with similar efficacy (eviQ CML; HSANZ).

explanation.summary: Chronic phase CML is treated with a BCR-ABL1 TKI - imatinib is standard first-line; transplant is reserved for advanced or refractory disease. Monitoring uses serial BCR-ABL1 transcript PCR by International Scale (eviQ).
key_points: Diagnosis: t(9;22) Philadelphia / BCR-ABL1
First-line: TKI (imatinib 400 mg, or 2nd-gen)
Monitor with BCR-ABL1 PCR transcript
Transplant for blast crisis or TKI failure
explanation.pearls: Basophilia on the differential is a clue to CML - rare in other myeloproliferative neoplasms.

stem: A 68-year-old man with stage A (Binet) CLL diagnosed 18 months ago has been on watch-and-wait. His lymphocyte count has been stable around 18-22 x10^9/L. He attends with three weeks of unilateral right cervical lymph node enlargement that is now 5 cm, firm and rapidly growing. He has lost 6 kg in the last month, has drenching night sweats and a temperature of 38.4 deg C this morning. His Hb is 102 g/L (was 132), LDH is 920 U/L (was 220) and uric acid is 0.48 mmol/L. He has no infection elsewhere. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis shows bulky cervical, mediastinal and abdominal lymphadenopathy with a dominant 5 x 4 cm right cervical node. PET-CT shows the dominant node has SUVmax 18 with significantly higher avidity than other nodes. He has not had previous chemotherapy. He has not received rituximab. His original CLL FISH showed del(13q) only. He has no concurrent infection on cultures.

data_table.Examination: 5 cm firm right cervical lymph node, generalised lymphadenopathy, 3 cm splenomegaly.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102 g/L (was 132), WCC 24 (lymphocytes 18), platelets 142, LDH 920 (was 220), uric acid 0.48, beta-2 microglobulin elevated, blood film smudge cells with some larger atypical cells, PET-CT dominant node SUVmax 18.

lead_in: Which finding most strongly suggests Richter transformation requiring biopsy?

options.text: Lymphocyte count rising from 18 to 22 x10^9/L over 18 months
options.rationale: Slow lymphocyte rise is expected in untreated CLL and does NOT indicate transformation; doubling time matters more than absolute count (BSH CLL Guideline).

options.text: Smudge cells on blood film
options.rationale: Smudge cells are characteristic of CLL itself, not transformation (BSH CLL).

options.text: PET avidity SUVmax >10 in a dominant node alongside rising LDH and B symptoms
options.rationale: Richter transformation (CLL evolving to aggressive lymphoma, usually diffuse large B-cell) is suggested by rapidly enlarging asymmetric nodes, B symptoms, marked LDH rise and PET SUVmax >10 in a dominant node. Biopsy of the most FDG-avid node is required for diagnosis (BSH CLL Guideline; eviQ).

options.text: Hb 102 g/L alone
options.rationale: Mild anaemia can reflect marrow infiltration in progressive CLL without indicating transformation; combined with other features it may, but in isolation it is non-specific (BSH CLL).

options.text: Original FISH showing del(13q)
options.rationale: Isolated del(13q) is a favourable prognostic marker in CLL and is not predictive of Richter transformation; del(17p) and TP53 mutation carry higher transformation risk (BSH CLL).

explanation.summary: Richter transformation should be suspected in CLL patients with rapidly enlarging asymmetric nodes, B symptoms, sharply rising LDH and PET SUVmax >10 in a dominant node; excisional biopsy of the most FDG-avid node confirms diagnosis (BSH CLL Guideline).
key_points: Watch-and-wait: stable CLL without symptoms, cytopenias or bulk
Treatment triggers: B symptoms, progressive cytopenias, bulky/symptomatic nodes, doubling time <6 months
Richter transformation: ~5-10% lifetime risk
Biopsy most FDG-avid node
explanation.pearls: del(17p) and TP53 mutations predict Richter risk and require targeted agents (BTK/BCL2 inhibitors) rather than chemoimmunotherapy.

stem: A 24-year-old previously well woman presents with a painless 4 cm left supraclavicular lymph node enlarging over 3 months. She reports drenching night sweats, intermittent fevers above 38 deg C and 8 kg unintentional weight loss over 6 weeks. She has noticed itching, particularly after warm showers. She does not drink alcohol but on the rare occasions she has, the node becomes painful within minutes. There is no infection source on history. Examination reveals firm supraclavicular and bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy, mild splenomegaly and no skin rash. Excisional lymph node biopsy is performed and immunohistochemistry shows large bi-nucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic nucleoli, expressing CD30 and CD15, negative for CD20, set in a mixed inflammatory infiltrate. PET-CT shows supradiaphragmatic-only involvement (left supraclavicular, mediastinal, both cervical nodal regions). FBC, LDH, ESR and HIV screen are pending but she is haemodynamically stable. She is keen to understand her diagnosis and likely treatment intensity. She has no past pregnancies and no significant comorbidities.

data_table.Examination: 4 cm firm left supraclavicular lymph node, bilateral cervical nodes, mild splenomegaly, no rash.
data_table.Investigations: Excisional node biopsy: CD30+ CD15+ CD20- bi-nucleate Reed-Sternberg cells in mixed inflammatory infiltrate. PET-CT supradiaphragmatic only.

lead_in: Which feature most specifically supports a diagnosis of classical Hodgkin lymphoma over a non-Hodgkin lymphoma?

options.text: Biopsy showing CD30+ CD15+ CD20- Reed-Sternberg cells in mixed inflammatory infiltrate
options.rationale: Reed-Sternberg cells with the classical CD30+ CD15+ CD20- immunophenotype set in a mixed inflammatory background are diagnostic of classical Hodgkin lymphoma; NHL lacks Reed-Sternberg cells and typically expresses CD20 (B-cell) or T-cell markers (BSH Hodgkin; eviQ).

options.text: Reproducible alcohol-induced pain in the affected lymph node region after ingestion
options.rationale: Alcohol-induced node pain is a classic clinical association of Hodgkin lymphoma but it is rare (<10%) and not specific enough to outweigh immunohistochemistry; it is suggestive but not diagnostic (BSH Hodgkin Guideline).

options.text: Drenching night sweats with documented unintentional weight loss exceeding 10 percent baseline
options.rationale: B symptoms (fever, night sweats, >10% weight loss) occur in both Hodgkin and NHL and do not distinguish between them (BSH Hodgkin; eviQ).

options.text: Painless rubbery supraclavicular lymph node confirmed enlarged on bedside clinical examination
options.rationale: Painless lymphadenopathy occurs in both Hodgkin and NHL and is not discriminating (BSH Hodgkin).

options.text: PET-CT showing supradiaphragmatic-only nodal involvement consistent with early-stage disease distribution
options.rationale: Contiguous nodal spread above the diaphragm is more typical of Hodgkin but NHL can present similarly; pattern alone is not diagnostic without histology (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Diagnosis of Hodgkin lymphoma rests on Reed-Sternberg cells with the CD30+ CD15+ CD20- immunophenotype in a mixed inflammatory background; clinical features (alcohol-induced pain, contiguous spread) are supportive but not diagnostic (BSH Hodgkin; eviQ).
key_points: Reed-Sternberg = Hodgkin (classical)
CD30+ CD15+ CD20- in classical Hodgkin
B symptoms not discriminating between HL and NHL
Stage by Ann Arbor; A/B for absence/presence of B symptoms
explanation.pearls: Bimodal age distribution (15-35 and >50) is characteristic of Hodgkin.

stem: A 70-year-old man is referred for investigation of back pain and a 12 g/L drop in haemoglobin over 6 months. Pain is constant in the mid-thoracic spine, worse at night, with no clear mechanical trigger. He has lost 4 kg unintentionally over 3 months. He has had two episodes of pneumonia in the last 12 months. His past history includes hypertension. Examination reveals tenderness over the T6 vertebra without neurological deficit. Bloods show Hb 92 g/L, MCV 88 fL (normocytic), creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28; baseline 78), corrected calcium 2.78 mmol/L (RR 2.10-2.60), albumin 28 g/L, total protein 92 g/L. Serum protein electrophoresis shows an IgG kappa paraprotein of 42 g/L. Serum free light chain kappa is 320 mg/L with kappa/lambda ratio 28. Urinary Bence Jones protein is positive. Skeletal survey and low-dose whole body CT show multiple lytic lesions in the skull, spine and pelvis with a compression fracture at T6. Bone marrow aspirate is pending. He has no neurological signs of cord compression. There is no infection currently. He has no MGUS surveillance recorded.

data_table.Examination: T6 spinal tenderness, no neurological deficit, no peripheral neuropathy.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 92 g/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28), corrected Ca 2.78, albumin 28, IgG kappa paraprotein 42 g/L, FLC kappa 320 mg/L (ratio 28), urinary BJP positive, lytic lesions on CT, compression fx at T6.

lead_in: Which feature is NOT one of the CRAB criteria defining symptomatic multiple myeloma?

options.text: Corrected calcium 2.78 mmol/L (>2.75)
options.rationale: Hypercalcaemia (corrected calcium >2.75 or >0.25 above ULN) is the 'C' in CRAB (IMWG).
source_refs: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma

options.text: Creatinine 198 micromol/L (eGFR 28)
options.rationale: Renal impairment (creatinine >177 or CrCl <40) is the 'R' in CRAB (IMWG).
source_refs: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma

options.text: Serum paraprotein concentration of 42 g/L
options.rationale: CRAB criteria are hyperCalcaemia, Renal impairment, Anaemia and Bone lesions; paraprotein concentration is NOT part of CRAB. The size of paraprotein helps distinguish MGUS (<30 g/L) from smouldering/active myeloma but the diagnostic CRAB criteria are end-organ damage (IMWG; HSANZ).
source_refs: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma
HSANZ position statement on multiple myeloma

options.text: Hb 92 g/L (below baseline by >20 g/L)
options.rationale: Anaemia (Hb <100 or fall >20 below baseline) is the 'A' in CRAB (IMWG).
source_refs: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma

options.text: Lytic lesions and a compression fracture at T6
options.rationale: Bone lesions on skeletal survey, CT, MRI or PET are the 'B' in CRAB (IMWG).
source_refs: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma

explanation.summary: CRAB criteria define active myeloma: hyperCalcaemia >2.75, Renal impairment (Cr >177 or CrCl <40), Anaemia (Hb <100), Bone lesions. The 2014 update added SLiM criteria: clonal plasma cells >=60%, FLC ratio >=100, >1 focal lesion on MRI. Paraprotein size separates MGUS from smouldering myeloma but is not CRAB (IMWG).
key_points: CRAB = Calcium, Renal, Anaemia, Bone
SLiM = >=60% plasma cells, FLC ratio >=100, >1 MRI focal lesion
MGUS = paraprotein <30, no CRAB, plasma cells <10%
Smouldering = paraprotein >=30 OR plasma cells 10-60%, no CRAB
explanation.pearls: Back pain + anaemia + raised Ca + raised creatinine in an older adult = myeloma until proven otherwise.

sources.label: IMWG Diagnostic Criteria for Multiple Myeloma
HSANZ position statement on multiple myeloma

stem: A 38-year-old man is admitted with newly diagnosed Burkitt lymphoma with extensive abdominal disease, bulky retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy and bone marrow involvement. He is haemodynamically stable. Pre-chemotherapy bloods show urate 0.62 mmol/L (RR 0.20-0.42), potassium 4.6, phosphate 1.6 mmol/L, calcium 2.30, creatinine 92 (eGFR 86), LDH 4800 U/L (>10x upper limit), WCC 14 with 8% blasts on film. He has no known G6PD deficiency but has not been formally tested. He is not pregnant. He is to start intensive R-CODOX-M/IVAC chemotherapy within 24 hours. He has no significant cardiac history. He is in a tertiary haematology unit with continuous monitoring. He has good IV access. He has no allopurinol allergy. The haematology team classifies him as high risk for tumour lysis syndrome by Cairo-Bishop criteria. He has no contraindication to IV hydration. The unit has rasburicase available. Renal output is currently 80 mL/hr. He weighs 75 kg.

data_table.Examination: Bulky abdominal mass, otherwise well.
data_table.Investigations: Urate 0.62 mmol/L, K 4.6, phosphate 1.6, corrected Ca 2.30, creatinine 92, LDH 4800, WCC 14 with 8% blasts.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate prophylactic strategy for tumour lysis syndrome in this high-risk patient?

options.text: Rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg IV daily plus IV hydration 3 L/m^2/day with close electrolyte monitoring
options.rationale: High-risk TLS (Burkitt, raised baseline urate, LDH >10x ULN) is managed with rasburicase (recombinant urate oxidase) which actively breaks down existing urate, generous IV hydration to maintain urine output >100 mL/m^2/hr and 6-8 hourly electrolyte monitoring during the first 72 hours. Screen for G6PD deficiency first - rasburicase is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency (BSH TLS; eviQ).

options.text: Allopurinol 300 mg daily and IV fluid hydration 2 L/m^2/day, without any rasburicase use
options.rationale: Allopurinol is adequate for intermediate-risk TLS; for HIGH-risk (Burkitt, ALL with high WCC, LDH >2x ULN with bulky disease) rasburicase is recommended because allopurinol does not clear existing urate (Cairo-Bishop; BSH TLS Guideline; eviQ).

options.text: Urinary alkalinisation with intravenous sodium bicarbonate infusion targeting urine pH above 7.5 consistently
options.rationale: Routine urinary alkalinisation is no longer recommended; it increases calcium phosphate precipitation in tubules and offers no clear benefit when rasburicase is used (BSH TLS).

options.text: Delay chemotherapy by 7 days to allow oral allopurinol to reduce baseline serum urate substantially
options.rationale: Burkitt lymphoma has a doubling time of 24-48 hours; delaying chemotherapy for prophylaxis is inappropriate; rasburicase normalises urate within hours (eviQ).

options.text: Prophylactic upfront haemodialysis arranged via nephrology prior to commencing the first chemotherapy cycle
options.rationale: Dialysis is reserved for established TLS with refractory hyperkalaemia, hyperphosphataemia or oliguria; not prophylactic (BSH TLS).

explanation.summary: High-risk TLS (Burkitt, ALL with WCC >100, raised baseline urate, LDH >10x ULN) requires rasburicase plus generous IV hydration plus 6-8 hourly electrolyte monitoring. Screen for G6PD before rasburicase. Allopurinol is for intermediate risk (BSH TLS; eviQ).
key_points: Cairo-Bishop laboratory TLS: 2+ of urate, K, phosphate, Ca derangements
High risk: Burkitt, ALL WCC >100, bulky LDH >10x ULN
Rasburicase: clears existing urate (not allopurinol)
Avoid rasburicase in G6PD deficiency
explanation.pearls: Routine alkalinisation is OUT - increases calcium phosphate crystallisation.

stem: A 54-year-old woman with AML on day 12 of induction chemotherapy presents to the haematology ward with a temperature of 38.5 deg C on two readings 30 minutes apart. She has had mild rigors. She has no localising symptoms. Her central venous catheter exit site is clean. Her oral mucosa shows grade 2 mucositis without obvious infection. Her last chemotherapy was cytarabine plus daunorubicin completed 8 days ago. She has no penicillin allergy. She has been on aciclovir and fluconazole prophylaxis. She has had no recent travel. She is haemodynamically stable. Her current bloods show neutrophil count 0.2 x10^9/L (severe neutropenia), Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 72, lactate 1.4, CRP 78. Blood cultures from the line and a peripheral site are drawn before any antibiotic is given. CXR is clear. Urine dipstick is negative. There is no diarrhoea or cough. She has good IV access. Her MASCC score is calculated as 18 (lower risk, but with mucositis). She has had no antibiotics in the last 90 days. She has no recent C. difficile.

data_table.Examination: Grade 2 oral mucositis, clean CVC exit site, no rash, no focal signs.
data_table.Investigations: Neutrophils 0.2 x10^9/L, Hb 92, platelets 38, creatinine 72, lactate 1.4, CRP 78, CXR clear, blood cultures sent.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate empirical antibiotic regimen?

options.text: Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate plus ciprofloxacin home outpatient regimen
options.rationale: Outpatient oral regimens are only appropriate for selected low-risk MASCC >=21 patients without mucositis, profound or prolonged neutropenia; this patient with grade 2 mucositis and neutrophils 0.2 is not eligible (eTG; eviQ).

options.text: Meropenem 1 g IV every 8 hours as first-line
options.rationale: Meropenem is reserved for second-line in persistent fever after 72-96 hours on first-line, septic shock or known ESBL/AmpC organisms - not first-line empirical therapy (eTG).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin plus caspofungin from the outset
options.rationale: Empirical vancomycin is not added routinely from the outset; reserved for suspected line infection, skin/soft tissue infection, haemodynamic instability, MRSA colonisation. Empirical antifungal is added if fever persists beyond 72-96 hours despite broad antibacterials (eTG; eviQ).

options.text: Ceftriaxone 1 g IV daily
options.rationale: Ceftriaxone lacks anti-pseudomonal cover and is inadequate empirical therapy in high-risk neutropenic sepsis (eTG).

options.text: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours after cultures
options.rationale: First-line empirical therapy for high-risk febrile neutropenia in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours (or cefepime); broad gram-negative cover including Pseudomonas. Cultures drawn first. Add vancomycin only if line infection, skin/soft tissue infection or haemodynamic instability suspected (eTG Antibiotic; eviQ).

explanation.summary: Empirical therapy for high-risk febrile neutropenia in Australia is piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g IV every 6 hours (or cefepime if pip-tazo unavailable) after cultures. Antifungal added if fever persists beyond 72-96 hours. Vancomycin only when line/SSTI/instability suspected (eTG; eviQ).
key_points: Define: T >=38.5 once or >=38.0 over 1 hour with neutrophils <0.5
First-line: pip-tazo 4.5 g IV q6h (or cefepime)
Escalate to meropenem if persistent fever + shock
Add antifungal if fever persists 72-96 h
explanation.pearls: Always take cultures FIRST but never delay antibiotics beyond 60 minutes from recognition.

stem: A 64-year-old man with metastatic prostate cancer (PSA 480 ng/mL, known bony metastases) on androgen deprivation therapy presents to ED with 3 days of progressive mid-thoracic back pain that is worse at night and on coughing, and 12 hours of bilateral lower limb weakness. He noticed difficulty initiating micturition this morning. He has had 4 kg weight loss recently. He has no fevers and no constitutional features of infection. On examination he has marked tenderness over the T7-T8 spinous processes. Power is 3/5 bilaterally in hip flexion and knee extension, with brisk knee jerks, upgoing plantars and a sensory level at approximately T8. There is no saddle anaesthesia tested. The post-void bladder scan shows 580 mL retained urine. He is haemodynamically stable. He has not received recent radiotherapy. He has had no falls. He has no anticoagulation. He has no penicillin allergy. He is otherwise mentating normally. He arrived in ED 90 minutes ago. The on-call oncology team is contactable. He has good IV access. He has no contraindication to dexamethasone.

data_table.Examination: T7-T8 spinal tenderness, bilateral lower limb weakness 3/5, brisk reflexes, upgoing plantars, T8 sensory level, urinary retention 580 mL.
data_table.Investigations: PSA 480, FBC normal, U&Es normal, alk phos 320 U/L, calcium 2.45, INR 1.0.

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate immediate management step?

options.text: Plain spine X-rays in the emergency department and outpatient MRI booked within 1 week
options.rationale: Plain X-rays miss up to 20% of MSCC and outpatient MRI delays definitive treatment; any delay risks permanent paraplegia (eviQ; NICE).

options.text: Dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat AND urgent MRI of the whole spine within 24 hours
options.rationale: Suspected malignant spinal cord compression mandates immediate IV dexamethasone 16 mg (with PPI cover) and urgent MRI of the WHOLE spine within 24 hours (sooner if rapidly progressing) to plan definitive treatment (RT or surgery). Whole-spine MRI is essential because 30% of cases have multiple levels (eviQ MSCC; NICE).

options.text: Lumbar puncture with CSF analysis to rule out an infective transverse myelopathy first
options.rationale: LP is contraindicated when MSCC is suspected because of risk of coning and offers no diagnostic value over MRI (eviQ).

options.text: Commence opioid analgesia with regular paracetamol and review symptoms in clinic next week
options.rationale: MSCC is a time-critical oncological emergency; outpatient review will not preserve neurological function (NICE MSCC).

options.text: Whole-body bone scan to identify the lesion site before any definitive further management
options.rationale: Bone scan is for disease staging but is inadequate to evaluate cord compression; MRI is the imaging of choice (eviQ; NICE).

explanation.summary: Malignant spinal cord compression: dexamethasone 16 mg IV immediately + whole-spine MRI within 24 hours + urgent oncology/radiation oncology consultation. Definitive treatment is surgical decompression (if life expectancy >3 months and good performance status) or radiotherapy (eviQ; NICE).
key_points: Treat on clinical suspicion - don't wait for imaging to start dexamethasone
Whole-spine MRI - 30% have multilevel disease
Surgery if PS good and life expectancy >3 months
RT for most others
explanation.pearls: Pre-treatment ambulatory status is the strongest predictor of post-treatment ambulation - act fast.

stem: A 42-year-old previously well non-smoker presents to ED with one week of progressive facial swelling, neck swelling, dyspnoea and headache worse on bending forward. She has noticed dilated veins on her chest wall. She has had two episodes of pre-syncope in the last 24 hours. She has no haemoptysis or chest pain. On examination she has facial plethora, periorbital oedema, distended jugular veins to the angle of the jaw, dilated collateral veins on the anterior chest wall and a positive Pemberton sign. Stridor is absent at rest. There is no airway compromise currently but she is sitting forward in bed and saturating 95% on 2 L O2. CT chest with contrast shows a large anterior mediastinal mass compressing the SVC with extensive thrombus. She has not been previously diagnosed with malignancy. Her FBC, U&Es, LFTs and LDH (elevated at 950) are pending tissue diagnosis.

data_table.Examination: Facial plethora, periorbital oedema, distended JVPs, dilated chest wall collaterals, positive Pemberton sign, no stridor at rest.
data_table.Investigations: CT: large anterior mediastinal mass compressing SVC with thrombus; LDH 950; no tissue diagnosis yet.

lead_in: In the absence of airway compromise or critical neurological symptoms, which step is most important BEFORE starting empirical corticosteroids?

options.text: Start dexamethasone 16 mg IV stat regardless of biopsy timing
options.rationale: Empirical steroids before biopsy are appropriate only with airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms; otherwise they may obscure lymphoma histology (eviQ SVC).

options.text: Start mediastinal radiotherapy empirically before tissue diagnosis
options.rationale: Radiotherapy without histology obscures pathology, can render large areas uninterpretable, and the regimen depends on tumour type. Reserve for life-threatening compression with no other options (eviQ).

options.text: Obtain tissue diagnosis (core biopsy of accessible peripheral node or mediastinal biopsy)
options.rationale: In SVC obstruction without airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms, tissue diagnosis should be obtained BEFORE corticosteroids whenever possible because steroids can transiently shrink lymphoma and obscure histology, delaying definitive treatment by weeks. Anterior mediastinal mass + young non-smoker with high LDH = lymphoma until proven otherwise (eviQ SVC obstruction; BSH Lymphoma).

options.text: Therapeutic anticoagulation alone for the SVC thrombus
options.rationale: Anticoagulation may be added for thrombus, but it does not address the underlying tumour; tissue diagnosis remains the priority (eviQ).

options.text: Endovascular SVC stenting as definitive management
options.rationale: SVC stenting provides rapid symptomatic relief and is appropriate for refractory or critical SVCO, but it should not delay tissue diagnosis when the patient is clinically stable; histology guides chemotherapy choice (eviQ).

explanation.summary: SVC obstruction in a previously undiagnosed patient: get tissue diagnosis FIRST unless airway compromise or critical CNS symptoms force empirical steroids. Anterior mediastinal mass in a young non-smoker is lymphoma until proven otherwise, and lymphoma is curable with histology-directed chemotherapy (eviQ).
key_points: Stable SVCO: tissue diagnosis first
Critical SVCO (airway, CNS): empirical steroids +/- stent
Anterior mediastinal mass DDx: lymphoma, thymoma, teratoma, thyroid
Stenting and anticoagulation for thrombus
explanation.pearls: Steroids before biopsy can obscure lymphoma histology for weeks - delay treatment, not diagnosis.

stem: A 68-year-old woman with metastatic breast cancer (bony and visceral disease) on letrozole presents with 5 days of progressive lethargy, polyuria, polydipsia, constipation and nausea. She is now confused (GCS 13, normally GCS 15). Her family report poor oral intake for 3 days. She has no fever and no localising signs of infection. She has CKD with baseline creatinine 95 (eGFR 56). Bloods show corrected calcium 3.6 mmol/L (RR 2.10-2.60), creatinine 168 (eGFR 28), albumin 32, phosphate 0.84, magnesium 0.74, PTH suppressed at less than 0.5 pmol/L, PTHrP elevated, alkaline phosphatase 280, vitamin D 62. She is haemodynamically stable but mildly hypotensive at 102/64 with HR 104 reflecting dehydration. ECG shows shortened QT interval, no arrhythmia. She has no current anticoagulation. She has no recent dental work and no osteonecrosis history. She is otherwise mentating moderately. There is no constipation impacting bowel obstruction. She has good IV access. The renal team is contactable.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, mildly drowsy, confused, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected calcium 3.6, creatinine 168 (was 95), albumin 32, phosphate 0.84, Mg 0.74, PTH <0.5 (suppressed), PTHrP elevated, ALP 280, ECG short QT.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride 200-300 mL/hr titrated to urine output, then zoledronic acid 4 mg once euvolaemic
options.rationale: First-line for severe hypercalcaemia of malignancy is aggressive IV rehydration with 0.9% saline (3-6 L over 24 hours, titrated to urine output and cardiac tolerance) followed by IV bisphosphonate (zoledronic acid 4 mg over 15 minutes) once euvolaemic. Onset 2-4 days, nadir 4-7 days. Adjust zoledronic acid dose for renal impairment (eviQ Hypercalcaemia; eTG; AMH).

options.text: IV furosemide 80 mg before adequate fluid resuscitation to enhance urinary calcium excretion early
options.rationale: Loop diuretics promote calciuria but are NOT used until after adequate rehydration; giving them before fluids worsens dehydration and AKI (eviQ Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Subcutaneous calcitonin as sole monotherapy without bisphosphonate or any IV fluid resuscitation at all
options.rationale: Calcitonin provides rapid (4-6 h) but transient calcium-lowering (~0.5 mmol/L) and tachyphylaxis occurs by 48 hours; useful as a bridge alongside fluids and bisphosphonate but not as monotherapy (eviQ).

options.text: Oral prednisolone 40 mg daily without intravenous fluids or bisphosphonate therapy as first-line treatment
options.rationale: Glucocorticoids are useful in vitamin-D-driven hypercalcaemia (lymphoma, granulomatous) but NOT in PTHrP-driven malignancy (eviQ).

options.text: Haemodialysis arranged as the first-line treatment regardless of underlying renal function or volume status
options.rationale: Dialysis is reserved for severe hypercalcaemia refractory to fluids/bisphosphonates or when fluid overload prevents resuscitation; not first-line (eviQ).

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia of malignancy: aggressive IV saline first, then IV bisphosphonate once euvolaemic. Add calcitonin if profound or symptomatic to bridge the bisphosphonate onset. Denosumab is an alternative when bisphosphonate is contraindicated or in refractory cases (eviQ; eTG).
key_points: Saline first - then bisphosphonate
Zoledronic acid 4 mg IV over 15 minutes; adjust for renal function
Calcitonin for rapid bridging only (tachyphylaxis by 48 h)
Steroids for vitamin-D-mediated (lymphoma, granulomatous) only
explanation.pearls: PTHrP-driven hypercalcaemia is the breast/squamous lung phenotype - won't respond to steroids.

stem: A 56-year-old man with cirrhosis is admitted to ED with massive haematemesis. He has bled approximately 2.5 L by the time he arrives and is in haemorrhagic shock. He is haemodynamically unstable - BP 78/42, HR 142, peripherally shut down, GCS 13. The ED activates the massive transfusion protocol. He has received 6 units of packed red cells, 4 units of FFP and 1 pool of platelets over the last 60 minutes via rapid infuser. He is being continued on the MTP. Repeat bloods show Hb 78 g/L (after transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1 g/L, potassium 6.2 mmol/L, ionised calcium 0.84 mmol/L (RR 1.10-1.30), pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, base excess minus 12, magnesium 0.62. ECG shows tall T waves in V2-V4.

data_table.Examination: Cold peripheries, ongoing haematemesis, jaundice, ascites.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 g/L (post-transfusion), platelets 62, INR 2.1, fibrinogen 1.1, K 6.2, ionised Ca 0.84, pH 7.18, lactate 8.4, BE -12, Mg 0.62; ECG tall T waves V2-V4.

lead_in: Which is the single most important next intervention to address an immediately life-threatening complication of massive transfusion?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL (or calcium chloride 10% 10 mL via central access)
options.rationale: Citrate in stored blood products chelates ionised calcium causing acute hypocalcaemia that depresses myocardial function and worsens coagulopathy. Ionised calcium 0.84 is severe and immediately life-threatening when combined with ongoing transfusion. Calcium replacement (gluconate peripherally or chloride centrally) is the single most important next intervention; it also helps stabilise myocardium against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP; NBA Patient Blood Management).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate 100 mmol bolus to correct the metabolic acidosis and stabilise the membrane
options.rationale: Bicarbonate does not correct the underlying lactic acidosis of hypoperfusion; the priority is restoring perfusion, replacing calcium and addressing hyperkalaemia. Bicarbonate also acutely lowers ionised calcium, worsening the hypocalcaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Insulin 10 units IV with 50 mL of 50% dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly now
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose shifts potassium intracellularly and is reasonable for hyperkalaemia, but ionised calcium 0.84 with continuing massive transfusion is the more immediate life-threat because it impairs cardiac contractility and coagulation. Calcium also stabilises the membrane against the hyperkalaemia (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: Stop the massive transfusion protocol immediately pending haemodynamic improvement and clinical reassessment now
options.rationale: He remains in haemorrhagic shock with ongoing bleeding; stopping MTP risks exsanguination. Address metabolic complications IN PARALLEL with continued resuscitation (ANZSBT MTP).

options.text: IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes to reduce ongoing surgical site fibrinolytic bleeding
options.rationale: TXA should be given (within 3 hours of bleeding onset) for major haemorrhage and is part of MTP, but it does not address the immediate cardiac risk of ionised calcium 0.84 plus hyperkalaemia; calcium correction is more urgent for this stem (ANZSBT MTP).

explanation.summary: Massive transfusion complications cluster as the 'lethal triad' (acidosis, hypothermia, coagulopathy) plus citrate-induced hypocalcaemia and hyperkalaemia from stored cells. Calcium replacement is the most overlooked but critical intervention; ratio 1:1:1 of RBC:FFP:platelets reduces coagulopathy (ANZSBT MTP; NBA PBM).
key_points: Ratio 1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets
Replace calcium - citrate chelation is rapid and dangerous
Watch K+ from stored RBC; treat if symptomatic or >6.5
Add TXA (within 3 h) and target fibrinogen >1.5
explanation.pearls: Ionised calcium below 0.9 mmol/L is the silent killer of MTP - check and replace at every 4-unit interval.
